explicit_text: I was born in San Jose, California, on May 14, 1893. I remember once taking my mother to visit the site of my birth on Third Street near St. James Park, which had become the location of the Osen Motor Sales Company garage. San Jose was where my early life began before we moved to other places.

explicit_text: The Osen Motor Sales Company garage was located on the site where I was born in San Jose. Interestingly, at the same time I was working on putting a new floor in the Osen Motor Sales garage in Reno. It’s funny how that connection came full circle in my life.

explicit_text: Shortly after I was born, my family moved to Watsonville, California, where we lived with my mother's parents, the Hamms. I spent my early days swimming in the Pajaro River and stealing watermelons and strawberries from the local Chinese community. I also enjoyed watching boats come in from San Francisco to load fruits and vegetables.

explicit_text: My maternal grandfather, Jeremiah Hamm, lived in Watsonville and made his living training horses for the San Francisco Fire Department. He would sell the trained horses to the department, which was quite an important role back then. I remember him as part of my early family life in California.

explicit_text: We moved to Cripple Creek, Colorado, when I was quite young. My main memory of Cripple Creek is digging little holes and pretending to have a mine of my own. I don’t remember much else from that time, but it was an early stop on our family’s journey.

explicit_text: After Cripple Creek, we moved to Colorado Springs where my grandfather Edward Ferris lived. He ran a linen and silk business with locations in Colorado Springs and Philadelphia, and he traveled to Europe annually to buy linens. I have fond memories of playing pinochle with him and taking long walks to places like the Garden of the Gods.

explicit_text: While living in Colorado Springs, I often took long walks with my grandfather to the Garden of the Gods. I vividly remember being taken by my father to the top of the highest rock there, where I looked down a narrow ledge about nine miles down. It was an impressive and somewhat scary experience for me as a child.

explicit_text: During our time in Colorado Springs, the Antlers Hotel was the main hotel in town. I recall when it burned down in a huge fire that also spread to a nearby lumber yard. It was quite a spectacle and left a strong impression on me as a child.

explicit_text: We moved to Trinidad, Colorado, which was a coal mining town at the time. I remember witnessing a secret festival of the Penitentes, where they whipped each other with thorny twigs. Also, sledding down a steep hill to the railroad station was a favorite pastime, though it sometimes caused injuries due to icy sidewalks.

explicit_text: In Trinidad, Colorado, I saw the Penitentes, a group of Indians who held a yearly festival involving self-whipping with thorny twigs. It was a private event, but we kids hid in the sagebrush to watch from a distance. The red marks on their backs were quite a sight, though I wasn’t close enough to be affected.

explicit_text: We moved to Pacific Grove, California, where my father worked as a sign painter and played flute in the local orchestra. I learned to fish there and earned money cleaning fishermen’s boats. We played games all over the peninsula, often ending up at Carmel, and I remember the Carmel Mission as a deserted building at that time around 1905.

explicit_text: We moved to Reno when I was in the eighth grade, and I attended Southside School and later the old high school there. I worked on the floor of the Osen Motor Sales garage in Reno, which was a funny coincidence since the company’s garage was also on the site of my birth in San Jose. Reno was where I finished my schooling and had many memorable experiences.

explicit_text: Dr. Ed Dopey Chase was the principal of the high school I attended in Reno. After a prank involving an electric wire in the attic, I was blamed and had to study in his office and run messages for teachers. He was a memorable figure in my school days.

explicit_text: E. E. Winfrey was the principal at my high school in Reno. I once got into trouble with him after a wrestling match where I threw a water cooler at another student. He called me a whelp and an imp of Satan, which led to me hitting him and being barred from school temporarily.
